result:
Sometimes I think about the way light looks when it’s been traveling for eight minutes and twenty seconds just to slap the side of a brick wall. It’s older than the moment you’re seeing it, a breadcrumb from a furnace we happen to orbit. That light has no memory, no plan, no luggage—it just leaves, and whatever it touches is suddenly visible, claimed, warm. I like to imagine each photon as a tourist that never goes home, a one-way traveler who dies on impact with your retina, giving its last scrap of energy so you can say “ah, there’s the wall.”  

I’m writing this on a train that smells of copper and croissants. Across the aisle a kid is pressing his forehead to the window, breathing little kidney-shaped ghosts on the glass, then wiping them away with the heel of his palm—create, erase, create, erase—like a private tide. He doesn’t know yet that this is the exact rhythm most adults use on their own memories: breathe a dream on it, rub it out, pretend the glass was always clear.  

Out beyond the window, the world is doing its unabashed green thing. Fields unzip themselves all the way to the horizon, revealing dark soil that looks wet and loved. A red tractor is a comma in the middle of a sentence no one is reading aloud. The clouds are stacked like untended paperwork. I keep waiting for one of them to stamp itself “URGENT” and drift faster, but they refuse to be bureaucratic; they just lounge, slow as Sundays.  

There’s a woman two seats ahead who keeps touching her own collarbones the way you’d check a pocket for keys—quick, habitual, half-reassuring. I invent a story: she’s flying to a hospital where someone’s heart is learning a new beat without her. The collarbone is the last place he kissed before the ambulance doors folded shut. She’s making sure the moment hasn’t slipped further down.  

I realize I’m doing the same with this page, touching it to be certain the words are still warm. We’re all just guarding small fires, palms cupped so the wind of the next minute doesn’t snuff them. Somewhere else, someone is arguing about algorithms, tariffs, whether a movie remake ruined their childhood. Meanwhile the planet tilts, breweries hum, and a spider whose name I’ll never know threads a guy-wire between two dandelions like she’s rigging a circus for ants.  

I used to think growing up meant trading wonder for accuracy—swap the invisible friend for the correct answer, exchange the dragon for the amortization table. But wonder wasn’t the casualty; velocity was. The faster you learn to categorize, the shorter the gap between “I don’t know” and “Oh, that,” and the less time the mind spends hovering in the aromatic middle, the place where anything could still be everything.  

So I’m practicing spraining my certainties. I leave a little extra silence after the barista asks for my name, let it wobble in the air like a coin that hasn’t landed. I walk home with my shoes untied on purpose, feel the laces flick wet punctuation marks onto my ankles. I stare at the night sky until the constellations unstick, until Orion’s belt droops like a guitar strap and the Pleiades scatter into sugar. It’s not regression; it’s renegotiation—an adult petition to keep one window slightly open for the inexplicable.  

The train slows. The kid’s latest ghost-fog is the biggest yet, a cloudy continent that covers half the pane. He pauses, fingers splayed, then instead of erasing it he draws a simple smile with his pinky—two dots, an upturned curve. After that he leaves it there to evaporate on its own timetable. I decide that’s today’s lesson: some breath-marks we don’t have to wipe away; we can let them fade while we watch, trusting that the glass will be clear again when we need it, and that the wall outside, older than the light that finds it, will still be there to receive whatever we choose to see next.
